assignment_description:
Solomon Carvalho: Solomon Joins Fremont
⍞ Materials: Westward with Fremont, notebook, United States Atlas, blank map

➜ PREP
Your geography notebook will be used on both days of the week. You may be asked to make an entry or to tape your printed maps for Map Work. Ensure that each entry is labeled with the date or lesson number. Your notebook should have a minimum of 70 pages. It is suggested to label the first page in your notebook “Solomon Carvalho's Journey” and set aside the next 5 pages. Place a marker or paper clip, label the page "John Audubon's Wilderness Travels," and save 5 pages. Place another marker. This section will be used on the second day for notebooking work; label the title page for that section “The United States of America.”
 
➜ INTRODUCTION   
Westward with Fremont: The Story of Solomon Carvalho tells the true story of a dangerous expedition led by explorer John C. Fremont and documented by photographer Solomon Nunes Carvalho. Carvalho was not a mountain man or soldier; he was an ordinary person and an artist. Armed with early photographic equipment, he traveled across the Great Plains, through the towering Rocky Mountains, and into the arid lands of the Great Basin, eventually reaching California. You will particularly study the river systems and mountain ranges traveled by the expedition. During the expedition, not all states were established; there were many regions they traveled through that were still considered territories.

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.27
1. In your United States Atlas, locate Washington, D.C., New York City (New York), and Baltimore (Maryland). These are major cities in the Eastern region of the country. Notice that Washington, D.C., is labeled as the District of Columbia on the map.
2. What states border the city of Washington, D.C.? (Maryland and Virginia) The city is not part of either state. It is the capital city of the country.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Westward with Fremont Ch.1-2 p.1-11 “I give you” - ”their Jewish neighbors.”

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: geography notebook, blank map of U.S.
∞ Map Link: Country Outline Map
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of U.S.
In your notebook, print and tape the blank map of the United States on the page after your “Solomon Carvalho's Journey” page. Notice that one map includes all the state outlines while the other does not. If you feel you need the state borders to guide you, then use the Blank Map of U.S. If you feel you want to place your own borders or territory sections, then choose the Country Outline Map. 
You will use this map to record Solomon’s journey throughout future lessons. Title the map “Solomon’s Journey West.” Locate Baltimore, Maryland. Draw a house to signify Solomon’s hometown. Now, locate the 38th parallel and draw a faint dotted line along the map. This will be the route the group will try to follow.

• DEFINITIONS
expedition: a journey taken for a specific purpose, often exploration

frontier: the edge of settled land

daguerreotype: an early type of photograph made on a metal plate

survey: to measure and map land

38th Parallel in the United States: passes through California (near San Francisco/Mono Lake), Nevada (Henderson/Hoover Dam), Arizona (Grand Canyon), Oklahoma (Tulsa), Missouri, Kentucky, and Virginia

Northeastern U.S.
⍞ Materials: Our States Have Crazy Shapes, United States Atlas, blank map, geography notebook

PREP: Print 8 copies of the linked United States blank map, to be used over the next several weeks. One of these is to be placed in your student's geography notebook (leave an opposite page blank for the state capitals). States will be added from week to week, and the map will be used to study each week. The additional maps are used at the beginning of each week's lesson to see what the student remembers.
[∞ Map Link: Blank Map of U.S.](https://www.geoguessr.com/assets/seterra/printables/en/us-states-map.pdf)

➜ INTRO
The second lesson each week in Geography will be spent on Map Questions and Map Work/Projects. There is a Read, Narrate, & Discuss portion from Our States Have Crazy Shapes that will cover two states per lesson in order of their acquired statehood. Please work for the time scheduled. There will be days when your state notebook entries might not be possible. Save those entries for another day or when you can return to them. It is recommended that you find a game about the states or follow along with the "Extra Helpings" of video suggestions to enlighten your assigned reading about each state.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.14-15, 27
1. Our stories this year are about the journey of Solomon Carvalho and the adventures of John James Audubon. They begin in the eastern part of the United States, but we will take you through to the West Coast of the United States. Day two will focus on each region of the United States. We will begin in the northeastern part and will work our way to fill the entire map of the United States.
2. Read p.14-15 about our "giant patchwork quilt."
3. Now turn to p.27 and locate Maine, New Hampshire, and Vermont. Add them to your blank map that you will place in your geography notebook.
4. What country borders these states to the North? (Canada) What Ocean borders the states to the east? (Atlantic Ocean) Add Canada and the Atlantic Ocean to your map.
5. Tape this map into your notebook. On the blank opposite page, start a state capital list. Add the state and capitals for Maine, New Hampshire, and Vermont to your list.
6. Spend some time studying the states, their capitals, and the region. Next week, you will fill these in from memory.

➜ READ & NOTEBOOK
Delaware and Pennsylvania
Our States Have Crazy Shapes Ch.1-2 p.33-39
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.33, 47
1. Draw an outline of Delaware and Pennsylvania on separate pages in your notebook in "The United States of America" section. Moving forward, any states that you draw will be in this section. Be sure to label each page with the state name at the top. Observe the boundaries, and try to answer the following questions: What geographical features make up the boundaries of the state? Is there anything unique about the borders?
2. Read the pages in the book for each state. You may want to read about each state at a time and work on the map for that state.
3. Add any comments to your maps that you learned about in the reading. Aim to include at least 3 interesting things about the state and its boundaries. See example below.
[∞ Image Link: Example of State Map](https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/jup0ft3fy1u3tfrdltcrv/Example-of-State-Map.pdf?rlkey=97xmw2r7d34npl11p5ijxwbnp&dl=0)

• EXTRA HELPING
View the video tour of Acadia National Park.
∞ Video Link: Acadia National Park

Solomon Carvalho: Solomon Joins Fremont
⍞ Materials: Westward with Fremont, geography notebook, United States Atlas, Rivers Map

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.101
1. Locate St. Louis on p.101.
2. Locate the Missouri River. The river starts in the Rocky Mountains in southwestern Montana and ends just north of St. Louis, Missouri. It runs about 2,341 miles and is the longest river in the U.S.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Westward with Fremont Ch.3-4 p.12-21 “Baltimore, with its” - ”turned everything silver.”

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: geography notebook
In the Solomon Carvalho section on the map of the U.S., use a color pen or pencil to draw the Missouri River on the blank map. You will be drawing several rivers during the study of this expedition. For reference, you may use the link to the Rivers Map to help you draw your own rivers on the blank map in your notebook. As you continue reading, you will notice how close the expedition stays to different river systems.
∞ Map Link: Rivers Map

• DEFINITIONS
provisions: supplies needed for a journey

terrain: physical features of the land

command: authority to lead

Northeastern U.S.
⍞ Materials: Our States Have Crazy Shapes, United States Atlas, blank map, geography notebook

PREP: Make sure you have a blank map of the U.S.
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of U.S.

➜ MAP WORK

2. Use the map in your notebook to check your work. Add anything you missed.
3. On p.27, locate the following states: New York, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and Massachusetts. Add these to the map in your notebook.
4. Add the states and capitals for New York, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and Massachusetts on the state capitals page.
5. Spend some time studying the states, their capitals, and the region. Next week, you will fill these in from memory.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.10-11
1. Looking at the northeastern region of the United States, what is the predominant climate zone? (Cold Wet in the far north and the lower states in the region are Temperate Wet)
2. Based on the Warming Up map on p.11, is the region experiencing a warming trend? (Yes, the Northeast is well above its usual temperatures for 2021.)

➜ READ & NOTEBOOK
New Jersey and Georgia
Our States Have Crazy Shapes Ch.3-4 p.41-48
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.43, 65

2. Read the pages in the book for each state. You may want to read about each state at a time and work on the map for that state.
3. Add any comments to your maps that you learned about in the reading. Aim to include at least 3 interesting things about the state and its boundaries. See example below.
∞ Image Link: Example of State Map

Solomon Carvalho: Westward from St. Louis 
⍞ Materials: Westward with Fremont, geography notebook, United States Atlas

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.89, 101
1. Locate Kansas City (Westport), MO, p.101. Westport was a trading post town during the time of the expedition. It was later annexed to be part of modern-day Kansas City, MO, in 1897.
2. Locate the Illinois River p.89. The river is part of the Illinois Waterway, connecting the Great Lakes to the Mississippi River, serving as a major commercial navigation route. It is about 273 miles long.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Westward with Fremont Ch.4-5 p.22-35 “Father sniffed at” - ”he said.”

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: geography notebook
On your map in the Solomon Carvalho section, use a color pen or pencil to draw the Illinois River. Now find Westport, MI, draw a dot, and label “Start of Expedition.”

• IMPORTANT DATES
Westward migration peak 1840s–1850s

• DEFINITIONS
territory: an organized division of a country that is not yet admitted to the full rights of a state

river basin: land drained by a river

migration: movement from one place to another

Northeastern U.S. Regional Features
⍞ Materials: Our States Have Crazy Shapes, United States Atlas, blank map, geography notebook

PREP: You will need two different maps for today—the U.S. and the Northeastern Region
∞ Map Link: Blank map of the Northeastern Region
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of the U.S.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.26-27
1. Using a fresh blank U.S. map, fill out the states, capitals, and the geographical features you remember.
2. Use the map in your notebook to check your work. Add anything you missed.
3. On your printed blank map of the northeastern region, add geographical physical features.
4. On the topographical/physical map (p.26), is there anything you notice about the geographical physical features of the northeastern region? What geographical features stand out?
5. Locate Lake Erie. What geographical features are near Lake Erie? Find the Erie Canal, Niagara Falls, and Lake Ontario. Label the new map.
6. Locate Massachusetts Bay. Find the following near the bay: Gulf of Maine, Cape Ann, Cape Cod, and Martha's Vineyard. Label the new map.
7. Locate the Hudson River and Appalachian Mountains. Label the new map.
8. Tape this map into your notebook and label it Northeast Region. You will add 4 other regional maps to your notebook throughout the year, so leave the next 4 pages empty for upcoming maps.
9. Spend some time studying the features you have added.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.26-27
1. Find the Appalachian Mountains. What other mountain ranges are close to the Appalachians in the northeastern region? (White Mountains, Allegheny Mountains, Catskill Mountains, Adirondack Mountains) The Appalachians stretch from the Island of Newfoundland in Canada to central Alabama in the United States. The range is about 2,050 mi (3,300 km).
2. What is the highest mountain in the northeastern region? (Mt. Washington at 6,288.2 ft/1,917 m in New Hampshire)

➜ READ & NOTEBOOK
Maryland and South Carolina
Our States Have Crazy Shapes Ch.7-8 p.57-63

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Westward with Fremont Ch.9-10 p.58-71 "Solomon Carvalho citizen" - "of their guards."

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: geography notebook
On your map, use a color pen or pencil to draw in the Republican River, the Arkansas River, the Rocky Mountains, and the Rio Grande River. Draw a dot at the bottom of Pikes Peak (one of the high mountain peaks of the Rockies) just north of the start of the Rio Grande River, label it New Year’s Day 1854. Join the labels on the map and/or the recollection of places you have read about, so that you can begin to visually track the expedition's journey with a line.

• DEFINITIONS
elevation: height above sea level

range: chain of mountains

➜ MAP WORK

• DEFINITIONS
current: a body of water or air moving in a definite direction, especially through a surrounding body of water or air in which there is less movement

glacier: a large, persistent body of dense ice, snow, rock, sediment, and often liquid water that originates on land and moves continuously down slope under the influence of its own weight and gravity

avalanche: a rapid, sudden, and violent downslope movement of a large mass of snow, ice, rock, soil, and other debris

2. View the Westward Expansion map on p.15. In what years were most southeastern states added to the United States? (mostly 1783, but also 1810-1821 and 1842)

Local map
⍞ Materials: local map, geography notebook

PREP: This lesson requires that you take a look at a local map of your area. There are some city or town websites that might have online versions of maps for you to view. However, if it is not easily found, Google Maps is an alternative source to use.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Local Map (suggest you use Google Maps)
1. Locate your street. (A parent will probably need to help.) Do you live within the city limits? Mark your street on the map.
2. Do you have a downtown or city center? If so, in what direction would you travel from your home to get there? Locate your local seat of government—city hall, town hall, or municipal building. Do you live north, south, east, or west of it?
3. How are the streets laid out in your city? Is any portion of your city laid out in a grid? (Older areas may utilize the grid. Newer sections may utilize cul-de-sacs. Other patterns like loop roads and diagonals may be used.) If so, what direction do the streets run? (north-south, east-west, etc.)
4. What are the streets named in this area? Do you see any “streets,” “avenues,” or “lanes”? What other street types (e.g., way, court, parkway) can you find? Is there a pattern for when these names are used (such as cul-de-sacs being “courts”)?
5. Locate your local police department, fire station, and hospital. How would you travel there from your home? Do you have more than one police station, fire station, or hospital? If so, mark where they are all located. Are they close to each other or far apart? What do you notice about how they are spaced?
6. Look for familiar places (grocery stores you go to, places of worship, friends' homes, etc.). How would you travel there from your home? Choose one of the familiar places you found and find at least 2 different routes to travel there from your home. Which one do you think is faster? Mark the familiar places on your map.
7. Locate any parks or outdoor areas in your city. Mark them on your map. What direction would you travel to get there? Are there any within walking distance from your home?

John Audubon: Beloved Boy
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, geography notebook, and map links

➜ PREP
As you recall from Lesson 1, you set aside pages in your notebook for these readings. You labeled the section "Audubon’s Wilderness Travels." You will also need a blank 1850s United States map to mark the places mentioned in your readings. 
∞ Map Link: 1850s U.S. Map

➜ INTRODUCTION
Term 3 will have you following the life of the naturalist John James Audubon as he explores the wilderness of America during the late 1800s, a time when the new frontier still had much to learn about the land’s wildlife.

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Materials: United States Atlas p.27, world map
∞Map Link: World Map
1. On the world map link, can you locate Haiti and France? The island of Haiti has two main languages: Haitian Creole and French. Due to its history of French colonization, the island still speaks and uses French in formal settings. The majority of people, however, speak Haitian Creole (French-influenced). 
2. Now look at p.27 in your United States Atlas. Locate Pennsylvania. What ocean is between the U.S. and France? (Atlantic Ocean)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
This Strange Wilderness Prologue-Ch.1 p.xv-7 “In the heat” - ”a great wilderness.”

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: geography notebook, 1850s U.S. map
Print the 1850s map. Cut along the border of the page. Tape or paste this page on your first blank page in the “Audubon’s Wilderness Travels” section.  You will be tracking the places he visits. Label this map, "Audubon's Travels".

• DEFINITIONS
observation: careful watching to collect information

naturalist: someone who studies plants and animals in nature

medium: the material used by an artist (e.g., watercolor)

field notes: short written records made during observation

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.10, 128
1. Find the Great Basin on p.128. What states are part of the Great Basin? (Nevada, half of Utah, and sections of Idaho, Wyoming, Oregon, and California) It is mostly defined by its hydrographic nature (the way the water flows). All the rain in the area evaporates into the ground or flows into lakes. The water does not find its way into bigger water outlets like the Gulf of Mexico (Gulf of America) or an Ocean.
2. If the water doesn't flow into the bigger outlet waters, where do you think the water ends up? (It drains into smaller basins/lakes such as the Great Salt Lake, Pyramid Lake, and the Humboldt Sink.)
3. What kind of ecosystem do you think the Great Basin sustains? (Desert)
4. Look at p.10. What is the climate like in that area? (Arid Steppe, Desert, Dry Summer)

The student will see the difficulty and hopefully appreciate even more how map-making is such a precise job through the mapping activity. Don't let it stress them out; they will have to guesstimate to an extent.

John Audubon: The Strange Wilderness, Then and Now
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, geography notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.15, 115
1. Locate Texas on the map. What states border Texas? (Louisiana, Arkansas, Oklahoma, New Mexico) 
2. Locate Missouri on the map. What states border Missouri? (Arkansas, Oklahoma, Kansas, Nebraska, Iowa, Illinois, Kentucky, and Tennessee)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.8-9 p.80-90 “All we cared” - ”nature around me.”

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: geography notebook
Using colored markers to outline the borders, outline the states of Texas and Missouri on your map. Write their names inside the borders. Use a different light shade of colored pencils to shade in each state. How colorful is your map? Are you impressed with all the places Audubon traveled to?

➜ WRITE
On the last page in your Audubon section, write a letter to Audubon reflecting on how you feel about his contribution to ornithology and geography.

• DEFINITIONS
legacy: something left behind for future generations

conservationist: a person working to protect nature

ethics: principles about right and wrong (introduce briefly: collecting vs. modern non‑lethal study)

citizen science: community volunteers contributing to scientific data (e.g., bird counts)

Clay model of state
⍞ Materials: United States Atlas, geography notebook, Model Magic clay (or you can make your own salt dough), cardboard or surface for model, paint, toothpicks or pins

➜ MAP WORK
You will spend today's lesson making a model of a state studied this term. You may want to print out a blank map to use as a guide. You can paint your model, put in pins with flags to mark certain areas, or add other creative features. We have included an example for inspiration.
